title: Expense self-approval: submitter can set themselves as the approver and approve their own claim
role: department_manager, hr_specialist, payroll_admin, recruiter (any non-employee staff role)
description: createExpenseSchema accepts a client-supplied approverEmployeeId with no check that it differs from the submitter. The approve mutation authorises the row's approverEmployeeId (or any HR role). A submitter who sets approverEmployeeId to their own employee id therefore becomes the approver of their own expense and can approve it. Plain 'employee' role is blocked from expense.approve by canAccessProcedure, but every staff role above employee (department_manager, hr_specialist, payroll_admin, recruiter) reaches approve via protectedProcedure.
expectedBehavior: An expense approver must be a different person from the submitter (actor != target). A self-referential approver should be rejected at submission, and approve must refuse when approverEmployeeId === the row's employeeId.
actualBehavior: No actor != target guard exists. approverEmployeeId is taken verbatim from input; approve only checks that the caller is the assigned approver or HR, so a self-assigned approver passes.

evidence.snippet: approverEmployeeId: uuidSchema.optional().describe("Line manager who should approve; defaults to the employee's manager at submission time"),

reproduction: As a department_manager, call expense.create with approverEmployeeId = own employeeId and amount = 5000. Then call expense.approve { id, action: 'approve' }. The row moves to 'approved' with no second person involved.
technicalRisk: State transitions to 'approved' with no independent actor; no compensating audit record.
businessRisk: Fraudulent reimbursement: an employee with a staff role can approve and (see BIZ-002) pay their own expenses up to the 1,000,000 SAR schema cap without any second signer.
recommendation: Reject approverEmployeeId === submitter at create; in approve, additionally block when existing.employeeId === existing.approverEmployeeId or when the acting HR user is the submitter. Enforce a two-person rule for any monetary approval.
workaround: Restrict approve/markPaid to a dedicated finance role and monitor the audit log for same-actor submit+approve pairs (not currently logged — see BIZ-003).

title: Expense markPaid is authorised by companyProcedure, letting non-finance roles pay out expenses
description: The markPaid mutation is documented as 'Only HR / super_admin can do this' but is guarded by companyProcedure, which only excludes the 'employee' and 'candidate' roles. Every other role, including recruiter (who has no finance function), can mark any approved expense as paid. There is no expenses:pay capability check. Combined with BIZ-001, a single department_manager can submit, approve, and pay one expense end-to-end.
expectedBehavior: Only a payroll/finance capability holder (e.g. payroll_admin, hr_manager, super_admin) should close out an expense as paid, and the payer should differ from the approver/submitter.
actualBehavior: companyProcedure permits department_manager, hr_specialist, payroll_admin and recruiter; the code comment does not match the guard.

evidence.snippet:
markPaid: companyProcedure
  .input(z.object({ id: z.string().uuid() }))
  .mutation(async ({ ctx, input }) => { ... status: "paid", paidAt: new Date() ... })

reproduction: As a recruiter, call expense.markPaid { id } for any approved expense. It succeeds and sets status='paid'.
technicalRisk: Financial state 'paid' reachable by roles outside finance; no capability gate.
businessRisk: Loss of segregation of duties on cash-out; a single actor can complete the whole approve+pay chain, enabling reimbursement fraud.
recommendation: Replace companyProcedure with requireCapability of a new 'expenses:pay' (grant only to payroll_admin/hr_manager/super_admin), and add a payer != approver != submitter check. Write an audit record on payout.

title: No audit trail on any expense mutation (create / approve / markPaid)
description: The audit helper writeAudit is used for payroll runs, reopens and settlements, but the expense router never calls it. Approvals and payouts of company money leave no who/when/old/new record, so the self-approval and over-broad-payout risks (BIZ-001/002) cannot be detected or investigated after the fact.
expectedBehavior: Every financial state change (approve, reject, pay) should write an audit_logs entry with actor, role, old/new status and amount, per the project's 'Audit log for all sensitive operations' rule.
actualBehavior: No writeAudit calls exist anywhere in expense.ts; the module is entirely un-audited.

evidence.snippet: export async function writeAudit(ctx: AuditableContext, entry: AuditEntry): Promise<void> { ... }

reproduction: Approve and mark an expense paid, then inspect audit_logs: no rows are written for the action.
technicalRisk: No non-repudiation for expense financial events.
businessRisk: Fraud and error are undetectable; fails internal-control and audit expectations for a payroll product.
recommendation: Add writeAudit to approve, reject and markPaid (and ideally create/cancel), recording actor, role, amount and status transition.
workaround: None at the application layer; rely on DB-level change data capture if available.

title: Leave balance can be over-drawn: balance is checked at submission but not re-validated at approval
description: The available-balance check runs only in create, against the balance at submission time. Two (or more) non-overlapping requests can each pass because the balance is not decremented until approval. At approval, adjustLeaveBalance decrements with Math.max(0, ...), which silently floors a negative balance to 0 instead of rejecting, so the employee is granted more paid leave than their entitlement.
expectedBehavior: Approval should re-validate the current balance and refuse (or partially grant) when the request exceeds the remaining balance; balance must never be allowed to go negative silently.
actualBehavior: No balance re-check at approval; decrement clamps to 0, hiding the over-draw.

evidence.snippet:
if (bal && requestedDays > parseNumeric(bal.balance)) {
  throw new TRPCError({ code: "BAD_REQUEST", ... }); }  // only in create
const next = Math.max(0, parseNumeric(bal.balance) + delta);  // floors negative to 0, hides over-draw

reproduction: With balance = 10 days, create request A (7 days) and request B (7 days, non-overlapping) — both pass the create check. Approve both. Final balance shows 0 instead of -4; employee took 14 of 10 days.
technicalRisk: Balance integrity is not conserved across the request lifecycle.
businessRisk: Unearned paid leave and inflated accrued-leave-payout liability at settlement.
recommendation: Move/duplicate the balance check into updateStatus at the approval boundary (reject if days > current balance) and remove the silent Math.max floor (or log when a floor would occur).
workaround: HR manually reconciles balances before approving stacked requests.

title: Leave approvals have no segregation of duties: an approver can approve their own leave
description: updateStatus authorises super_admin, hr_manager and department_manager to approve leave. There is no check that the approver is not the leave's own employee. A department_manager who heads their own department (dept scope check passes because their own department is in the managed set) can approve their own leave request; hr_manager/super_admin can approve their own outright.
expectedBehavior: The approver of a leave request must not be the requesting employee; self-approval should be blocked and routed to a higher authority.
actualBehavior: Only role and (for department_manager) department scope are checked; actor != subject is not.

evidence.snippet: updateStatus: requireRole("super_admin", "hr_manager", "department_manager") ...
if (ctx.user.role === "department_manager") { ... if (!deptId || !managed.includes(deptId)) throw FORBIDDEN }  // no actor != employee check

reproduction: As a department_manager whose own employee record sits in a department they head, create a leave request (bound to self) then call updateStatus { id, data:{ status:'approved' } }. It succeeds.
technicalRisk: Approval control is role-based only, not identity-aware.
businessRisk: Managers self-grant paid leave; weakens leave governance and downstream accrual/payout accuracy.
recommendation: In updateStatus, load the request's employeeId and reject when it equals the caller's employeeId (for the approval transition); require escalation to a higher approver.
workaround: Policy control + monitor approvedByUserId vs employee.

title: Leave requests can be back-dated: no lower bound on start date
description: createLeaveRequestSchema only enforces endDate >= startDate; there is no minimum date. A request can be dated entirely in the past, decrementing the prior period's balance (the decrement year is derived from startDate), which can conflict with already-closed attendance/payroll periods.
expectedBehavior: Back-dated leave beyond a configurable grace window should be rejected or require explicit HR override, and should not silently affect a closed period.
actualBehavior: Any past startDate is accepted; year is taken from startDate for the balance effect.

evidence.snippet: .refine((data) => data.endDate >= data.startDate, { message: "End date must be on or after start date", path: ["endDate"] });  // no min date
const year = new Date(current.startDate).getUTCFullYear();  // balance effect keyed to (possibly past) startDate

reproduction: Create a leave request with startDate/endDate in a prior month; it is accepted and, on approval, debits that year's balance.
businessRisk: Retroactive leave can mask absences or manipulate a closed period's records.
recommendation: Add a minimum-date rule (e.g. startDate >= today - N days) and block edits to closed periods; require HR override with audit for exceptions.
workaround: HR review of any request with a past start date.

title: Monthly leave accrual compounds: year-to-date days are added to the existing balance on every run
description: calculateAccrual computes daysAccrued = (daysAllowed/12) * monthsElapsed, where monthsElapsed = min(12, currentMonth+1) is the YEAR-TO-DATE month count, then sets newBalance = previousBalance + daysAccrued. Because the YTD figure is ADDED to the already-accrued balance every time the job runs, running the monthly cron each month compounds the balance to daysAllowed * (1+2+...+12)/12 = daysAllowed * 6.5 by December (e.g. 21 days -> ~136 days). Correct only if run exactly once per year.
expectedBehavior: A monthly accrual run should add one month's entitlement (daysAllowed/12) to the balance, and be idempotent per month (no double-credit if re-run).
actualBehavior: It adds the YTD accrual to the prior balance, so periodic runs over-accrue geometrically.

evidence.snippet: let newBalance = previousBalance + daysAccrued;  // adds YTD accrual on top of prior balance
const results = runMonthlyAccrual(leaveTypeContexts, employeeContexts, balanceContexts, { runForYear: targetYear });  // cron re-runs monthly

reproduction: Seed balance 0. Run runMonthlyAccrual in Feb (monthsElapsed=2) -> balance = daysAllowed*2/12. Run again in Mar (monthsElapsed=3) -> balance = prev + daysAllowed*3/12. Balance grows by the YTD amount each month.
technicalRisk: Non-idempotent accrual; balance diverges from entitlement.
businessRisk: Every employee's leave balance is inflated across all tenants, inflating paid-leave usage and accrued-leave-payout liability at settlement.
recommendation: Make the monthly path grant exactly one month (daysAccrued = monthlyAccrual) and add idempotency keyed to (employee, leaveType, year, month) so a re-run in the same month is a no-op; add unit tests asserting balance == entitlement after 12 monthly runs.
workaround: Run the accrual job at most once per year until fixed; verify balances manually.

title: Duplicate final settlement / EOSB: no uniqueness or status guard on settlement creation
description: settlement.create inserts a new final_settlements row on every call with no check for an existing settlement for the same employee, and the table has no unique constraint or 'status/paid' field. offboarding.initiate also inserts an independent final_settlements row. The same employee can therefore have multiple EOSB records, each a candidate for payout, with no idempotency or 'already settled' guard.
expectedBehavior: An employee should have at most one active final settlement; a repeat attempt should update the existing record or be rejected, and a settled/paid record must be immutable.
actualBehavior: Every create/initiate call inserts a fresh row; nothing prevents duplicates.

evidence.snippet: const [settlement] = await ctx.db.insert(schema.tenant.finalSettlements).values({ employeeId: input.employeeId, esbAmount: eosb.eosbAmount.toString(), ... }).returning();  // no existing-settlement check
const [settlement] = await ctx.db.insert(schema.tenant.finalSettlements).values({ employeeId: input.employeeId, exitReason: `${input.primaryReason} :: ...` }).returning();  // second independent row
export const finalSettlements = pgTable("final_settlements", { id: ..., employeeId: uuid(...).notNull()...  // no unique(employeeId), no status/paid column

reproduction: Call settlement.create for employee X twice (or create then offboarding.initiate). Two final_settlements rows exist for X, each with an EOSB amount.
technicalRisk: No idempotency on a high-value financial record.
businessRisk: Duplicate end-of-service payouts (potentially tens of thousands of SAR each) if downstream payment reads more than one row.
recommendation: Add a unique/active constraint on employeeId (or a settlement status), check for an existing settlement before insert, and make a paid settlement immutable. Consolidate offboarding.initiate to reuse/upsert the same row.
workaround: HR procedure to check for an existing settlement before creating one.

title: Payroll updateStatus allows state-machine shortcuts and an un-audited completed->cancelled that re-opens the period lock
description: updateStatus accepts any target status from updatePayrollRunSchema. It blocks completed -> (non-completed/non-cancelled) but still permits completed -> cancelled with no reason and no audit, and permits forward jumps such as draft -> completed that skip pre_check/ready. Because the PAY-010 period lock exempts 'cancelled' runs, cancelling a completed run re-opens the month for a brand-new run, enabling a second payroll for the same period. Unlike the dedicated reopen mutation, updateStatus writes no audit record.
expectedBehavior: Status transitions should follow the defined lifecycle (draft -> pre_check -> ready -> completed) with only the audited reopen able to unwind a completed run; any transition of a completed run should be audited.
actualBehavior: Arbitrary forward jumps allowed; completed -> cancelled allowed silently with no audit; re-opens the period lock.

evidence.snippet: if (current.status === "completed" && input.data.status !== "completed" && input.data.status !== "cancelled") { throw FORBIDDEN }  // completed->cancelled slips through, no audit
if (existingRun && existingRun.status !== "cancelled") { throw CONFLICT ... }  // a cancelled run no longer blocks a new run for the period

reproduction: On a completed run, call updateStatus { data:{ status:'cancelled' } } (succeeds, no audit). Then payroll.run.create for the same periodMonth succeeds because the prior run is 'cancelled'.
technicalRisk: Undefined/loose state machine; audit gap on a completed-run transition.
businessRisk: A completed month can be silently re-run, risking double payment, with no audit trail of the cancellation.
recommendation: Enforce explicit allowed transitions; route any completed-run change exclusively through the audited reopen (with reason); write an audit record for every status change; do not treat 'cancelled' as unlocking a period that already produced payslips.
workaround: Operationally forbid using updateStatus on completed runs; use reopen only.

title: payslip.create allows manual injection of arbitrary payslip amounts, bypassing the calculation engine and audit
description: payslip.create accepts a client array of payslips (basic, housing, netPay, gosi, etc.) and inserts them directly with no recomputation against the GOSI/orchestrator engine, no verification that the run exists or is open, and no audit entry. Amounts are only bounded by the schema (basic/netPay positive, others >= 0), so arbitrarily large net-pay figures can be written straight into a run.
expectedBehavior: Payslip amounts should be produced by the orchestrator (as run.create does) or, for manual corrections, validated against the engine and audited, tied to a run in an editable state.
actualBehavior: Raw client values are inserted verbatim; no engine cross-check, no audit.

evidence.snippet: netPay: z.number().positive().describe("Net pay after all calculations"),  // no upper bound / engine check

reproduction: Call payroll.payslip.create with a payslip whose netPay = 999999 for any employeeId/payrollRunId. It is inserted unchanged.
technicalRisk: Payslip figures can diverge from the statutory engine; no consistency guard.
businessRisk: Inflated or fabricated net pay and GOSI figures enter the payroll ledger with no audit trail.
recommendation: Remove or lock down the raw create path; require corrections to go through an audited, engine-validated adjustment tied to a non-completed run.
workaround: Disable payslip.create in the UI; only create payslips via run.create.

title: Compensation adjustment can be self-approved in a single call (client-supplied status + approvedById)
description: createCompensationAdjustmentSchema lets the caller set both status and approvedById directly, and the create mutation inserts them verbatim. An hr_manager can create a salary adjustment already flagged status='approved' with approvedById = self in one request, with no separate approval workflow, no actor != approver check, and no audit entry.
expectedBehavior: A compensation adjustment should be created as 'proposed', approved by a distinct authorised approver, and the approval should be audited; approvedById should be set by the server from the approving session, not the client.
actualBehavior: status and approvedById are accepted from the client and stored as-is; no approval step, no audit.

evidence.snippet: export const createCompensationAdjustmentSchema = z.object({ planId, employeeId, adjustmentType, currentValue, proposedValue, status: z.string().optional(), approvedById: z.string().uuid().optional(), ... });

create: requireRole("super_admin", "hr_manager").input(createCompensationAdjustmentSchema).mutation(async ({ ctx, input }) => { const [item] = await ctx.db.insert(schema.tenant.compensationAdjustments).values(input).returning(); return item; })

reproduction: As hr_manager, call retention.compensationAdjustment.create with status='approved', approvedById = own employee/user id, proposedValue = large amount. It is stored as an approved adjustment.
technicalRisk: Approval fields are client-controlled; no SoD.
businessRisk: Unilateral, un-audited salary increases; a manager can self-approve compensation changes.
recommendation: Force status='proposed' on create, ignore client approvedById, add a separate approve mutation requiring a different authorised approver, and audit the approval.
workaround: Restrict comp-adjustment approval to super_admin and review manually.

title: Offer outcome transitions (send/accept/decline) set status unconditionally with no state-machine guard
description: The offer send/accept/decline mutations each set the status directly by id with no check of the current status. An offer can be moved to 'accepted' without ever being 'sent', re-accepted after being declined, or flipped between accepted and declined, and update can set any status with side-effect timestamps. There is no enforced draft -> sent -> accepted/declined lifecycle.
expectedBehavior: Only a 'sent' offer should be acceptable/declinable; terminal states (accepted/declined) should be immutable; illegal transitions rejected.
actualBehavior: Status is overwritten unconditionally regardless of prior state.

evidence.snippet: accept: requireRole("super_admin", "hr_manager").input(z.object({ id: z.string().uuid() })).mutation(async ({ ctx, input }) => { const [offer] = await ctx.db.update(schema.tenant.offers).set({ status: "accepted", acceptedAt: new Date(), ... }).where(eq(...)).returning(); ... })  // no current-state check
decline: requireRole("super_admin", "hr_manager") ... .set({ status: "declined", declinedAt: new Date(), declineReason: input.reason, ... })  // can run even on an accepted offer

reproduction: Create an offer (status draft), call offer.accept -> accepted without being sent; then call offer.decline -> declined. Both succeed.
technicalRisk: Offer records can hold inconsistent state/timestamps.
businessRisk: Mis-recorded hiring outcomes; a declined offer can be flipped to accepted (or vice versa) with no guard, affecting onboarding/headcount reporting.
recommendation: Guard each transition on the required prior status and make accepted/declined terminal; reject illegal transitions with a clear error.
workaround: Operational discipline; review offer audit history.

title: Referral creation trusts client-supplied referrerEmployeeId and rewardAmount (self-crediting)
description: referral.create is a bare protectedProcedure and inserts input directly; createReferralSchema carries a required client-supplied referrerEmployeeId and an optional rewardAmount. A user can create a referral crediting any employee (including themselves) and propose the reward amount. The reward payout transition (status='reward_paid') is HR-gated via referral.update, so money is not moved directly, but the referrer-of-record and proposed amount are attacker-controlled.
expectedBehavior: referrerEmployeeId should default to the caller's own employee id (or be validated), and rewardAmount should be set by policy/HR, not the submitter.
actualBehavior: Both fields are taken verbatim from the client at create.

evidence.snippet: create: protectedProcedure.input(createReferralSchema).mutation(async ({ ctx, input }) => { const [referral] = await ctx.db.insert(schema.tenant.referrals).values(input).returning(); return referral; })
referrerEmployeeId: z.string().uuid(),  ...  rewardAmount: z.number().min(0).optional(),  // both client-supplied

reproduction: Call recruitment.referral.create with referrerEmployeeId = own id and rewardAmount = 10000. A referral crediting the caller is created (payout still requires HR).
technicalRisk: Referrer attribution and proposed reward are not server-authoritative.
businessRisk: Referral-bonus gaming if HR approves on trust; incorrect referrer-of-record.
recommendation: Default referrerEmployeeId to the session employee (mirror the recognition.create SEC-010 fix) and derive rewardAmount from the reward policy rather than the client.
workaround: HR verifies referrer and amount before marking reward_paid.

title: Manager can author their own performance review/response (no actor != subject check)
description: review.create and reviewResponse.create for a department_manager are gated only by assertManagesEmployee / assertManagesReview (department scope). A manager whose own employee record is in a department they head can create or respond to a performance review about themselves, since the scope check passes. There is no check that the reviewer differs from the review subject.
expectedBehavior: A manager should not author or score their own performance review; self as subject should be rejected and escalated.
actualBehavior: Only department-scope is enforced; actor != subject is not.

evidence.snippet: create: requireRole("super_admin", "hr_manager", "department_manager").input(createReviewSchema).mutation(async ({ ctx, input }) => { await assertManagesEmployee(ctx, input.employeeId); const [item] = await ctx.db.insert(schema.tenant.reviews).values(input).returning(); ... })

export async function assertManagesEmployee(ctx, employeeId) { if (ctx.user.role !== "department_manager") return; ... // passes when the subject is in a dept the caller heads, including the caller themselves }

reproduction: As a department_manager who is a member of a department they head, call review.create with employeeId = own id. It succeeds.
technicalRisk: Review authorship is scope-based, not identity-aware.
businessRisk: Managers can write favourable self-reviews, corrupting talent/comp decisions that rely on review data.
recommendation: Reject when input.employeeId (or the review's employeeId) equals the caller's employeeId; require a skip-level reviewer for a manager's own review.
workaround: HR review of any self-authored review rows.

title: Approval/payout/leave transitions lack an atomic status guard (race-condition double-processing)
description: State-changing updates read the current status, then update WHERE id = ... (not WHERE id AND status = expected). Two concurrent requests can both pass the status check and both apply the transition (e.g. approve+approve, or markPaid twice, or approve leave twice decrementing the balance twice). The guards are read-then-write rather than a conditional atomic update.
expectedBehavior: Transitions should use an atomic conditional update (WHERE id AND status = expected) or a transaction/row lock so only one concurrent actor can perform the transition.
actualBehavior: Read-check-then-unconditional-update by id; no atomic guard.

evidence.snippet: const [updated] = await ctx.db.update(schema.tenant.expenses).set({ status: "paid", paidAt: new Date() }).where(eq(schema.tenant.expenses.id, input.id)).returning();  // WHERE id only
const [request] = await ctx.db.update(schema.tenant.leaveRequests).set(input.data).where(eq(schema.tenant.leaveRequests.id, input.id)).returning();  // then balance is adjusted; no atomic guard

reproduction: Fire two concurrent expense.markPaid calls for the same approved expense; both can read 'approved' before either writes 'paid', so both proceed (and any downstream payment fires twice).
technicalRisk: Non-atomic transitions permit double-processing under concurrency.
businessRisk: Potential double payment / double balance decrement under race conditions.
recommendation: Use conditional updates (.where(and(eq(id), eq(status, expected)))) and treat a zero-row result as 'already processed'; wrap balance-affecting leave approval in a transaction.
workaround: Low real-world likelihood at current scale; prioritise after the higher-severity SoD/calc items.
